ocr.text: In the context of hepatitis B virus (HBV) research, the examination of nucleotide mutations is critical for understanding the
virus's evolution and its implications for treatment efficacy. The data table presented herein summarizes the frequency of
specific nucleotide mutations, focusing on two distinct genotypes: Genotype A and Genotype D. Notably, the column labeled
"Nucleotide mutation" lists various amino acid substitutions in the HBsAg protein, which are known to play a significant role in
immune escape and vaccine resistance. Among the mutations documented, a total of 11 occurrences (equating to 14% of the
sampled cases) were identified for Genotype A, while Genotype D exhibited no recorded mutations in this category.
Nucleotide mutation  Genotype A  Genotype D
Such findings underscore the genetic variability within HBV strains and highlight the need for ongoing surveillance of these
substitutions, as they may influence both the clinical management of HBV infections and the development of effective
therapeutic strategies. The implications of these mutations extend beyond individual patient care, impacting public health
initiatives aimed at controlling HBV transmission and improving vaccination programs. Thus, understanding the genetic
landscape of HBV remains essential for enhancing treatment outcomes and guiding future research directions.
